provenance.title: Approximating SPR Distance Between Phylogenetic Trees with Graph Neural Networks
affiliations: ISEL – Instituto Superior de Engenharia de Lisboa, Instituto Politécnico de Lisboa, Portugal
provenance.abstract: Comparing phylogenetic tree topologies is essential for understanding epidemic dynamics, yet biologically meaningful distances such as the Subtree Prune and Regraft (SPR) distance are NP-hard to compute and intractable on large datasets. We investigate whether a Graph Neural Network (GNN) can approximate SPR distances in near-constant time per comparison after training. Our contributions are fourfold: a reproducible dataset of 864 phylogenetic trees, a validated pre-processing pipeline, a validation of the phangorn heuristic against exact rooted SPR, and a Siamese GIN regressor.
key_claims: A reproducible pre-processing pipeline that slices genomic profiles into size-stratified sub-datasets, diversifies them via controlled shuffling, infers UPGMA/NJ trees, applies midpoint re-rooting, and extracts SPR supervision labels.
A publicly released dataset of 864 trees and 388 labelled tree pairs across four bacterial species, with isolates up to 9,500 sequences.

A validation of the phangorn::SPR.dist heuristic supervision target against the exact rooted SPR distance computed with rspr (Pearson 0.98-0.99), showing it is a biased but excellent monotonic surrogate.
A Siamese Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN) regressor achieving R^2 ~= 0.87-0.90 in-distribution, with partial cross-species transfer (R^2 ~= 0.37) and failed size extrapolation (R^2 < 0).

architecture.primary_variant: Siamese GIN Regressor (2-layer GIN encoder + MLP regression head)

modules.name: NewickParser
modules.notes: Parsed with Biopython. Converted to bidirectional graph in PyTorch Geometric by duplicating each edge (u->v and v->u) so messages propagate both directions.

modules.name: NodeFeatureExtractor
modules.notes: Three of the four raw per-node features; used directly (not embedded).

modules.name: TaxonomicEmbeddingLayer

parameters.vocab_size: max_id + 1 (dynamic, to handle large isolate counts)

modules.notes: Trainable nn.Embedding mapping the 4th raw feature (categorical taxonomic identifier) to a 16-dim vector.

modules.name: FeatureConcatenation
modules.notes: Final per-node input feature vector fed into the GIN encoder.

modules.name: GINLayer_1
modules.notes: Shared-weight encoder applied identically to both trees of a pair (Siamese).

modules.name: GINLayer_2
modules.notes: AMBIGUITY: body text (Sec 4.2) states 'we stack two GIN layers', but Fig. 5 depicts three sequential GINConv/BatchNorm/ReLU blocks (Layer 1, Layer 2, Layer 3) per tree branch. This module represents the second of the two layers per the text account; see ambiguities[] for the third-layer discrepancy.

modules.name: GlobalAddPooling
modules.notes: Aggregates all N node embeddings of one tree into a single fixed-size graph embedding v_g. Authors note sum pooling gave best results among strategies tested, but is a source of size-related calibration bias.

modules.name: PairConcatenation
modules.notes: Concatenates the two Siamese tree embeddings v_a and v_b for the pair.

modules.name: MLPRegressionHead
modules.notes: Outputs the predicted (unrooted-heuristic-supervised) SPR distance for the pair. Prediction is clamped at zero at inference/training since negative SPR distances are biologically meaningless.

connections.tensor_name: v_g (tree A and tree B, shared encoder) [128] each

differences.note: Authors tested multiple pooling strategies; sum pooling reported as best-performing and used in the primary variant. Alternative pooling schemes not fully specified in the paper.

mathematical_spec.name: Huber loss (training objective)
hyperparameters: delta (threshold, not specified by paper — standard Huber form assumed)
undefined_symbols: delta (not given an explicit numeric value in the text)

lr_schedule.type: ReduceLROnPlateau (halve LR after 10 epochs without validation improvement); early stopping after 25 such epochs

data_augmentation: Controlled seeded-permutation shuffling of input profile row order (pre-processing stage, generates alternative UPGMA/NJ topological variants — not a training-time augmentation)

datasets.name: cgMLST profiles: Clostridium, Salmonella, Vibrio (EnteroBase) + Streptococcus pneumoniae (PubMLST)
datasets.split: 70/15/15 train/validation/test over 388 labelled tree pairs (seed 42), derived from 864 inferred trees

reported_results.dataset: in-distribution (4 species, mixed sizes, single split)
in-distribution (4 species, mixed sizes, single split)
in-distribution (4 species, mixed sizes, single split)

reported_results.metric: Pearson correlation (heuristic vs. exact rooted SPR)
Pearson correlation (heuristic vs. exact rooted SPR)
Pearson correlation (heuristic vs. exact rooted SPR)

evaluation_protocol.compute: Labelling-pipeline scalability stress-tested on a VM with 240 GiB RAM and Intel Xeon Silver CPUs (scaled to trees up to 9,500 profiles); GIN training compute (GPU/CPU, hardware, wall-clock time) not specified.
special_conditions: Predicted distances are clamped at zero at inference (negative SPR distances are biologically meaningless).

phangorn::SPR.dist heuristic (unrooted) is used as the supervision target rather than the exact rooted SPR distance from rspr, because exact computation is NP-hard and intractable for large trees; the heuristic systematically underestimates the exact rooted magnitude (~65-71% of exact value on validated small trees) despite near-perfect rank correlation.

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Adam optimizer beta1/beta2 use PyTorch defaults (0.9, 0.999) since the paper only specifies learning rate (1e-4) and weight decay (1e-4).
alternatives: custom beta values reported elsewhere by the authors (e.g. in released code, not in the paper text)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Batch size is not stated anywhere in the paper; assume a small batch size (e.g. 16-32 tree pairs) typical for a 388-pair dataset trained with GNN pair-encoders.
implementation_assumptions.basis: common practice for small paired-graph datasets
alternatives: full-batch training (272 training pairs per epoch)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Maximum training epochs assumed to be on the order of 100-200 given the described early-stopping patience of 25 epochs without improvement and LR halving after 10.
alternatives: no explicit epoch cap, purely early-stopping driven

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Huber loss delta (threshold parameter) assumed to be the PyTorch/standard default of 1.0, since the paper names the loss but does not give its threshold value.
alternatives: a tuned delta value scaled to the right-skewed SPR distance range (tens to low thousands)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: The categorical 'taxonomic identifier' feature is assumed to encode per-species (or per-source-dataset) membership rather than per-isolate identity, consistent with 'species id' labelling in Fig. 4's caption.
implementation_assumptions.basis: inferred from Figure 4 caption ('species id') vs. body text ('categorical taxonomic identifier')
alternatives: per-isolate unique identifier (would explain the dynamically-sized vocabulary of max_id+1 for isolate counts up to 9,500)

ambiguities.description: Body text states the GIN encoder 'stack[s] two GIN layers of hidden dimension 128', but Figure 5 explicitly diagrams three sequential GINConv/BatchNorm1d/ReLU blocks per branch, labelled 'Layer 1', 'Layer 2', 'Layer 3', each producing an [N,128] output before global add pooling.
Batch size for training is never stated; only the 70/15/15 pair-level split (388 total pairs) and optimizer/schedule hyperparameters are given.
Total number of training epochs/steps is not stated explicitly; only the LR-plateau (10 epochs) and early-stopping (25 epochs) patience windows are given.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: training runs until early stopping triggers, no fixed epoch cap stated

ambiguities.description: The Huber loss delta (transition threshold between quadratic and linear regions) is never given a numeric value in the text.
alternatives: a custom delta tuned to the SPR-distance scale (values range roughly 0-2000)
